properties.Description: Nishizu indicates that there was an early language school class started in the Talbert area by the parents of the earliest Japanese Talbert pioneers. It began in 1912, but it is not clear how long it existed. Nishizu does say that it was expanded around 1925 and included teacher's residences. Some of the people who taught here included Reverends Terahata, Junzo Nakamura, Oikawa, Watanabe, Kenji Kikuchi, Takahashi, and Sekiguchi.
The first teacher was Tokio Tanaka. This was the school that Nishizu attended. Initially, the class was taught in a small house, but later on, in 1925, a proper building was built.
First teacher was Mr. Nishimura, then Mr and Mrs. Imamoto and Mr. Chogyozi. Nishizu says that this location was used as a meeting place for multiple community functions.

properties.Location: Laguna Beach (Crystal Cove) Japanese Language School
properties.Description: In 1929, those families that moved to the Crystal Cove area decided to create a language and culture school. A local farmer, Shinichi Matsuyama, started the school at his home, although he had no children of his own. Reverend Kikuchi and his wife, Yukiko Kikuchi, were the first teachers of the school. As such, the school focused significantly on Christian teachings.

properties.Location: Laguna Beach (Crystal Cove) Japanese Language School
properties.Description: In 1932, the Crystal Cove public school building was made available for language school, where Reverend Kikuchi taught on Saturdays.

properties.Location: Laguna Beach (Crystal Cove) Japanese Language School
properties.Description: In 1935, the Japanese community created a new building on the west side of the coast highway for the language building; it also functioned as a community building. It was located on the bluff over crystal cove, although it is unclear where. What is known as "Cottage #34" (the Japanese language building) was moved to this location a few years after World War 2.
The First Teacher of the El Modena language school was Noriko Saruwatari.
The El Modena language school was moved to Irvine because of the large influx of Japanese people on the Irvine Ranch

Reverend Sohei Kowta of the Wintersberg Presbyterian Church and a "Mrs. Nitta" from Gardena first taught at the school. Reverend R.T. Takayama eventually took over from Reverend Kowta. The building was in fact rented from the school district.

Eventually another school was built on a parcel of land given to Mr. Ikeda by Mr. and Mrs. Carl Spencer. (Mrs. Carl Spencer, Fanny Weston Bixby Spencer was an anti-militarist, socialist, philanthropist, who according to Nishizu, assisted Japanese in the area in significant ways. According to Nishizu, the school was located "on the west side of Whittier Avenue, between 18th and 19th streets...about a mile west of Newport Blvd." According to "Tosh" Ikeda, via Nishizu, the location of the school was later used for mobile homes which were temporary quarters for returning incarcerated Japanese.
The school was founded by Suematsu Murata, and the principal and teacher was Shimomura Higa.

Nishizu indicates that there was an early language school class started in the Talbert area by the parents of the earliest Japanese Talbert pioneers. It began in 1912, but it is not clear how long it existed. Nishizu does say that it was expanded around 1925 and included teacher's residences. Some of the people who taught here included Reverends Terahata, Junzo Nakamura, Oikawa, Watanabe, Kenji Kikuchi, Takahashi, and Sekiguchi.
The first teacher was Tokio Tanaka. This was the school that Nishizu attended. Initially, the class was taught in a small house, but later on, in 1925, a proper building was built.
First teacher was Mr. Nishimura, then Mr and Mrs. Imamoto and Mr. Chogyozi. Nishizu says that this location was used as a meeting place for multiple community functions.

In 1929, those families that moved to the Crystal Cove area decided to create a language and culture school. A local farmer, Shinichi Matsuyama, started the school at his home, although he had no children of his own. Reverend Kikuchi and his wife, Yukiko Kikuchi, were the first teachers of the school. As such, the school focused significantly on Christian teachings.
In 1932, the Crystal Cove public school building was made available for language school, where Reverend Kikuchi taught on Saturdays.

In 1935, the Japanese community created a new building on the west side of the coast highway for the language building; it also functioned as a community building. It was located on the bluff over crystal cove, although it is unclear where. What is known as "Cottage #34" (the Japanese language building) was moved to this location a few years after World War 2.
The First Teacher of the El Modena language school was Noriko Saruwatari.
The El Modena language school was moved to Irvine because of the large influx of Japanese people on the Irvine Ranch

Reverend Sohei Kowta of the Wintersberg Presbyterian Church and a "Mrs. Nitta" from Gardena first taught at the school. Reverend R.T. Takayama eventually took over from Reverend Kowta. The building was in fact rented from the school district.

Eventually another school was built on a parcel of land given to Mr. Ikeda by Mr. and Mrs. Carl Spencer. (Mrs. Carl Spencer, Fanny Weston Bixby Spencer was an anti-militarist, socialist, philanthropist, who according to Nishizu, assisted Japanese in the area in significant ways. According to Nishizu, the school was located "on the west side of Whittier Avenue, between 18th and 19th streets...about a mile west of Newport Blvd." According to "Tosh" Ikeda, via Nishizu, the location of the school was later used for mobile homes which were temporary quarters for returning incarcerated Japanese.
The school was founded by Suematsu Murata, and the principal and teacher was Shimomura Higa.
